questions.question: Define Big O notation and explain its significance in algorithm analysis.
keyPoints: Definition: f(n) = O(g(n)) means there exist c, n₀ > 0 such that 0 ≤ f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀
Represents upper bound/worst-case complexity
Used to express maximum time or space an algorithm requires
Enables comparison of algorithms independent of hardware
Simplifies analysis by ignoring constants and lower-order terms

questions.question: Differentiate between Big O, Big Omega, and Big Theta notations with examples.
keyPoints: Big O (Ω): Upper bound - worst-case guarantee
Big Omega (Ω): Lower bound - best-case performance
Big Theta (Θ): Tight bound - both upper and lower are same
Example: f(n) = 3n² + 5n + 2 is O(n²), Ω(n²), and Θ(n²)
Each serves different analysis purposes

questions.question: Explain the concept of Worst-Case, Best-Case, and Average-Case analysis with an example.
keyPoints: Worst-case: Maximum resources needed for any input (e.g., element not found in linear search)
Best-case: Minimum resources for favorable input (e.g., element at first position)
Average-case: Expected resources over all possible inputs (assumes uniform distribution)
Example with linear search: Best O(1), Worst O(n), Average O(n)
Each provides different insights into algorithm behavior

questions.question: What is Space Complexity? Differentiate between auxiliary space and total space with an example.
keyPoints: Space complexity: Memory required by algorithm as function of input size
Auxiliary space: Extra space excluding input (temporary variables, recursion stack)
Total space: Input space + auxiliary space
Example: For array sorting in-place O(1) auxiliary, but O(n) total including input
Important for memory-constrained systems

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing order and explain: O(1), O(n), O(log n), O(n²), O(2ⁿ), O(n!)
keyPoints: Order: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) < O(n!)
O(1): Constant time (array access)
O(log n): Logarithmic (binary search)
O(n): Linear (linear search)
O(n²): Quadratic (bubble sort)
O(2ⁿ): Exponential (recursive Fibonacci)
O(n!): Factorial (permutation generation)

questions.question: Define input size and explain how it is measured for different problem types.
keyPoints: Input size (n): Measure of problem instance quantity
For arrays/sequences: number of elements
For strings: number of characters
For graphs: number of vertices and edges
For matrices: dimensions (rows × columns)
Choice affects complexity analysis significantly

questions.question: Explain why we use asymptotic notation instead of exact running time measurements.
keyPoints: Hardware-independent: Not affected by processor speed
Language-independent: Not affected by programming language
Scalability: Shows how algorithm grows with input
Simplicity: Ignores constants and lower-order terms
Comparison: Enables theoretical comparison of algorithms
Focus on essential growth rate rather than implementation details
Time complexity analysis: Outer loop runs n times, inner loop runs i times for each i
Total iterations = 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2 = n²/2 + n/2
Ignoring constants: O(n²) - quadratic time
Space complexity: Only uses variables i, j - constant space O(1)
No additional data structures created proportional to n
Thus: Time O(n²), Space O(1)

questions.question: Explain the analysis framework for algorithms with detailed explanation of asymptotic notations, their mathematical definitions, and when to use each.
keyPoints: Introduction: Need for algorithm analysis framework
Big O Definition: f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for n ≥ n₀ (Upper bound/Worst)
Big Omega Definition: f(n) ≥ c·g(n) for n ≥ n₀ (Lower bound/Best)
Big Theta Definition: c₁·g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c₂·g(n) (Tight bound)
Use O when guaranteeing maximum performance
Use Ω when describing minimum capability
Use Θ when upper and lower bounds are same
Examples and properties: transitivity, reflexivity

questions.question: A software company needs to process 1000 records. Compare algorithms with complexities O(n), O(n²), and O(log n) in terms of operations required. If n doubles, how does each scale?
keyPoints: For n=1000: O(log n) ≈ 10 operations, O(n) = 1000 operations, O(n²) = 1,000,000 operations
When n doubles from 1000 to 2000:
O(log n): log(2000)/log(1000) ≈ 1.1 times (minimal increase)
O(n): 2000/1000 = 2 times (linear increase)
O(n²): 4,000,000/1,000,000 = 4 times (quadratic increase)
Conclusion: O(log n) scales best for large n, O(n²) becomes impractical
Recommendation depends on n and performance requirements

questions.question: Define recurrence relation. Solve T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n and determine its complexity using recurrence tree method.
keyPoints: Recurrence relation: Equation defining function in terms of smaller inputs
Given: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n
Recurrence tree method:
Level 0: n
Level 1: 2 × (n/2) = n (two subproblems each cost n/2)
Level 2: 4 × (n/4) = n
Each level costs n, total levels = log₂n
Total cost = n × log n = n log n
Therefore T(n) = Θ(n log n) - this is merge sort complexity
